lead: Choosing the right removals firm is one of those decisions that quietly shapes how the whole moving day feels. After running our family business from Westerfield since 2005, we've learned that the customers who ask good questions up front are almost always the ones who end up relaxed and smiling when the last box comes off the van. So here, openly and from our own side of the conversation, are the questions worth putting to any mover before you commit, along with what a genuinely good answer should sound like.

sections.h: Will Someone Actually Survey Your Home Before Quoting?
sections.body:
The very first thing we'd encourage you to ask is whether the firm offers a proper survey before giving you a figure. By that we mean someone either visiting in person or carrying out a careful video walk-through, room by room, rather than guessing from a single line about a "three-bed semi". A quote built on assumptions is a quote that tends to wobble on the day, and nobody enjoys a surprise when the van's already half loaded.

In our experience, a survey is where the real planning starts. We're not just counting boxes; we're noting the wardrobe that won't fit down the stairs, the loft that's quietly stuffed, and the garden shed everyone forgets about until the morning of. A firm willing to spend that time with you is a firm that takes accuracy seriously. If a mover seems reluctant to look properly at what's actually moving, treat that as your answer.

The survey is also the natural moment to raise access at both ends. Mention the narrow lane, the third-floor flat without a lift, the long footpath from the road, or the parking that fills up by eight in the morning. The more we know early, the smoother the day runs, and a good firm will welcome those details rather than wave them away.

sections.h: What's Included in the Written Quote and What Isn't?
sections.body:
Once you've had a survey, the next question is simple but important: can I have everything in writing, with a clear breakdown of what's covered? A good written quote should spell out the labour, the vehicle, the time allowed, and any materials, so there's no quiet gap waiting to catch you out later. We always prefer customers to read ours line by line and ask about anything that looks unclear.

Just as useful is asking what isn't included. Some firms quote a tempting headline price and then add on packing materials, parking suspensions, or extra crew once they arrive. We'd rather tell you about a likely cost up front than spring it on you mid-move. When you compare quotes, make sure you're comparing like for like, because the cheapest figure on paper is rarely the cheapest once the extras are added.

Ask, too, how the quote behaves if the job turns out larger than expected. An honest mover will explain their approach calmly rather than leaving it vague.

While you have the quote in front of you, it's worth asking about payment terms in the same breath. Find out whether a deposit is required to hold your date, how much it is, when the balance falls due, and which payment methods are accepted. We think it's far better to settle all of that well before moving day, so that the only thing left to think about when the van pulls up is getting your family safely into the new home.

sections.h: How Are Your Belongings Protected Along the Way?
sections.body:
Few things matter more than knowing your possessions are looked after, so ask plainly how protection works. A trustworthy firm will explain that your goods are fully covered while in their care, talk you through the level of cover, and be happy to confirm it in writing. If the answer is woolly or rushed, push gently for detail, because this is exactly the part you want clear before anything is lifted.

Beyond the paperwork, ask how they physically protect things on the day. We use proper transit blankets, floor and door protectors, and sturdy packing materials precisely because most damage happens not in transit but in the careless moment of carrying something through a doorway. The practical answers a firm gives here often tell you more than any policy document about how much they genuinely care.

It's also fair to ask what happens in the unlikely event something does go wrong. We'd far rather a customer knows in advance how a claim is handled, who they speak to, and how quickly we aim to put things right. A firm that talks openly about the rare bad day, rather than pretending it never happens, is usually one that has thought hard about getting the good days right.

sections.h: Who Are the Crew and How Experienced Are They?
sections.body:
It's perfectly reasonable to ask how many people will turn up and how experienced they are. The size of the team should match the size of the job; too few and the day drags painfully, too many and you may be paying for hands you don't need. We work this out at the survey stage, and we're always happy to explain the reasoning behind the number we've suggested.

Experience is the quieter factor that makes a real difference. Lifting heavy furniture down a tight Suffolk staircase without marking the walls is a skill, not a fluke, and it's the sort of thing our crews have built up over many years together. Ask whether the team are the firm's own people or casual labour brought in for the day. There's nothing wrong with a busy firm, but you'll want to know who's actually handling your home.

We'd also gently suggest asking how long the firm has been trading and whether the same faces tend to return year after year. Longevity isn't everything, but a settled team that has worked together for a long time has usually ironed out the little problems that catch newer outfits off guard. That continuity is one of the quiet advantages of a family business, and it shows in how calmly a day unfolds.

sections.h: Do They Handle Packing, Dismantling and the Awkward Items?
sections.body:
Plenty of stress on moving day comes from the jobs people don't think to ask about. So check early whether the firm offers packing, and whether they'll dismantle and reassemble beds, wardrobes and flat-pack furniture at the other end. We offer full and part packing because we've seen how a kitchen packed properly the day before can transform the whole experience the following morning.

Then there are the awkward items: the piano, the American fridge-freezer, the heavy garden gym, the glass-topped table that everyone's nervous about. Ask specifically about anything unusual you own, rather than assuming it'll be fine. A capable mover will tell you straight away whether it's routine for them or needs special handling, and they'll have the right equipment and the right number of hands ready rather than improvising on the day.

Reassembly is the part people most often overlook, yet it makes an enormous difference to that first evening in a new place. Ask plainly whether beds will be rebuilt and wardrobes put back together, so that you're not hunting for an allen key at midnight. We make a point of getting the essentials reassembled before we leave, because a made-up bed at the end of a long day is worth more than almost anything else we can do.

sections.h: What Happens if Completion Dates Slip?
sections.body:
Anyone who has moved through a property chain knows that dates have a habit of moving at the last minute. That's why one of the smartest questions you can ask is what happens if your completion slips. A reassuring answer will cover both flexibility and storage, because sometimes the keys simply aren't ready when you'd hoped and your belongings need somewhere safe to wait.

We keep secure storage at our Westerfield base for exactly these moments, whether it's an overnight gap or a few weeks while a chain sorts itself out. Ask how a delay affects the cost, how much notice the firm needs to rearrange, and whether your goods stay fully covered while in store. Knowing all this before you book means a slipped date becomes a minor inconvenience rather than a crisis.

Finally, ask who you can call on the day and whether you'll have a single point of contact. A name and a working number, rather than a switchboard, is one of the simplest signs that you're dealing with a firm that wants the move to go well as much as you do.

faqs: Should I always get an in-person or video survey before booking?

We'd strongly recommend it. A proper survey, whether in person or by video, lets the firm see exactly what's moving and gives you a quote that holds up on the day rather than one based on guesswork. It also flags awkward access, large items and easily forgotten spaces like lofts and sheds, so there are far fewer surprises when the van arrives.
What should I check about how my belongings are protected?

Ask the firm to confirm in writing that your goods are fully covered while in their care, and to explain the level of that cover clearly. It's also worth asking how they physically protect things on the day, such as transit blankets, floor protectors and quality packing materials, since most damage happens during careful handling rather than in transit.
Can a removals firm store my things if my completion date slips?

A good firm should be able to. We keep secure storage at our Westerfield base for exactly these situations, whether it's a single overnight gap or several weeks while a chain settles. Ask how a delay affects the cost, how much notice is needed to rearrange, and whether your belongings remain fully covered while they're in store.
How many crew should turn up for my move?

The number should match the size and complexity of your move, which is something a survey helps the firm judge accurately. Too few and the day runs long; too many and you may be paying for hands you don't need. Ask whether the team are the firm's own experienced staff, and don't hesitate to ask why a particular crew size has been suggested.
